speaker: interviewer
text: The date is the-- September the 4th, 1996. The survivor is George Ginsburg. The interviewer is Dina Brustman. We are in Melbourne, Australia. And the interview will be conducted in English. My name is Dina Brustman. I'm interviewing George Ginsburg. The date is the 4th of September, 1996. And the interview is being conducted in Melbourne, Australia.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell us your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is George Ginsburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: G-I-N-S-B-U-R-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: George Ewsey Ginsburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell your middle name?

speaker: interviewee
text: E-W-S-E-Y, Ewsey.

speaker: interviewer
text: The date of your birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: 25th of February, 1923.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the place of your birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Danzig Free State.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: D-A-Z-I-N-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: Free state.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a free state. It's-- It-- it was independent.

speaker: interviewer
text: But just spell it for us, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: F-R-E-E S-T-A-T-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your name-- what is your age now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Too old.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to begin by talking a bit about your prewar life. Could you tell us some of your remembrances of your prewar life?

speaker: interviewee
text: My prewar life. Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was born in Danzig from Russian parents which escaped after the Russian Revolution via Poland to Danzig. My father's name was Mark. My mother's name was Sonia. They escaped via Poland to Danzig, where I was born. I was born in Sopot, Langfuhr, which is a holiday resort about 40 kilometers out of Danzig, on the beach. And I was born there only. But after two years, my parents left Danzig for Berlin. And I went to school in Berlin, in the state school. And we had a-- we had a-- a Russian nanny with us who also they took the way. And she spoke Russian with me all the time while I was in Berlin. My father learned German. So did my mother. My mother spoke French before, and German. And I was-- I grew up in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us a bit about your school. What kind of a school was it? And who were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went first-- I went first to the state school, and then into a higher school, a high school, called gymnasium. I was there for one year. And then I was thrown out of the gymnasium. Now, the reason was I was not-- my parents were not German. They were stateless. They had no nationality because their Russian passport was taken away from them and they were stateless. And the stateless people were the first one who got thrown out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was already in 193-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 7.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before 1937, tell us a bit about what life was like in Berlin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember being a-- a toddler in 1933, when the-- when about 100,000 Nazis with torches marched through the city. That was before the final election of Hitler as Reichskanzler. And as I looked, my father-- he was rather a very tall man. And tears were rolling down his-- and I said, dad, what are you crying for? He said, that's the finish of us. That's the finish of Germany. And they-- they-- they were singing that the blood shall flow from the knives of the Germans. Jewish blood shall flow. That's what-- that's a song. Like, I know it in German, but it's hard to translate. And then, well, I went to school. I had no trouble in school with the children at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No not at that stage. Not when I was small.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were your friends? Can you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. One particular friend was Walter, a-- a non-Jew German. And another one was Peter. And one was Werner Hirschfeld, who is here in Australia. We all lived in the same district. We all lived in 28 Regensburger Strasse, Berlin Charlottenburg, which was a good district like [INAUDIBLE] or-- you know, a very good district.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did many Jews live in that area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they did. Many Jews lived in that area. You see, the Jews were-- the Jews in Germany were actually part of Germany. They did not live particular, you know? And they were part of the culture and part of everything for-- for hundreds and hundreds of years. And they were considered-- the German Jews considered themselves as German first, and then Mosaic beliefs, you know, which is Jewish, after. Very national minded. And they contributed a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a-- a real estate agent, what you call. In German, they call Immobilien. You know, Immobilien. And he was selling and buying houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he working for himself or for someone else?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a commissioned agent, a independent commissioned agent, and had connections with other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was in the home, looking after me and looking after the home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your home like? Describe it for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: My home was a-- a-- a big apartment. On the first floor, we had two bedrooms-- big bedrooms with high ceilings. We had a large room, a dining room. We had two bathrooms. And we had a-- a beautiful balcony and a cedar door to walk into. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And did anyone--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had my own room. And we were, you know-- it's-- it was nice trees in that street and nice people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were your neighbors? Were they Jewish neighbors or gentile neighbors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my neighbor was a-- a Jewish fellow called Hirschfeld-- Hirschfeld family, who came from the country. They had stores, big stores. And the rest was non-Jewish. And opposite, there were Jewish people. I-- I used to go on a lot with Jewish people because I knew them. Some of them are now in England, some of them, Israel, and so on. And some of them are dead. But I used to go to a normal state school. And, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- was there anyone else living in the apartment with you, with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was our apartment. Belonged to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you brought a-- a nanny from Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: She lived with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was the only person--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --helping your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were no other--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us a little bit more about the school you went to. Tell us a bit more about the kind of studies you did at that school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went first-- the first high school I can remember-- because some of them is a bit, you know, faded since events of the war took over. And the school I went to is Fichte-Gymnasium in Emser Strasse, which was-- we were wearing red student caps. And the faculty of each [INAUDIBLE] we are studying was depending on the ribbon who was around that cap. Like, if it was a lilac ribbon, it was going for medicine. If it was a blue ribbon, it was going for something else. [INAUDIBLE]. And there were only two-- two-- two Jewish students in the whole class, me and another guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were you studying? What ribbon?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that particular time, I was just studying the basics. But what I wanted to really do is go to aeronautic engineering. But we have been suffering a bit-- started to suffer as a Jew in that school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you a good student?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I tried to be, because the teacher said always, about Ginsberg. You're so clever. You're supposed to be so clever a race. Tell me the answer. And-- and the whole class was laughing, you know? And I had to get up and give him the answer. And then outside, next to me were-- I was sitting with Hitler Youth brownshirts, the shorts and little daggers, going to school. And that's what I had to put up with.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us a little bit more about your family life. What did your parents do in their free time, on the weekends?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that particular time, we were-- we were actually starting to get restricted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which time was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1937. The Jew--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, tell us-- tell us before 1937 first, a bit more about how your family spent their time, and then how things changed.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father used to go out on the main street in Kurfuerstendamm, which was one of the biggest streets in Berlin. And business was done in coffeehouses, you know? In huge, big coffeehouses with-- with-- with cake and-- and-- and coffee. And the real estate agents used to come to one big-- big coffeehouse. And they used to do business there, selling and buying. Another coffeehouse was restricted for other traits, and professions, and things like that. So he used to go out every day into town. And my mother was home. And then in the evening, sometime they went out in the theater, or live show, or concert.

speaker: interviewer
text: They enjoyed music?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they did. My mother was very musical. She was-- she was studying in the conservatorium in Kiev. She went to the conservatorium in Kiev when she was 17, 18 years of age.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why didn't she continue with that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because the Russian Revolution started in Russia. And then-- and then they were still young. And they got out in 1922--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --after Russian Revolution.

speaker: interviewer
text: A little bit more of that your family life at home before 1937. Tell me about a Shabbat at your place.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we had Shabbat. We had the candles and we had the challah. And we had Shabbat. And after that, well, that was just the-- the three of us, so-- because I'm the only-- I'm the only son.

speaker: interviewer
text: What--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm the only child in the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about extended family members? Were there any grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandparents from my mother's side were living in Berlin first. And then they went out to Czechoslovakia, to Prague. So we had no family anymore in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did they leave for Prague?

speaker: interviewer
text: But before that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] '35.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we'll still focus on before 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you had a Shabbat, did your grandparents ever come?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes. Naturally.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you have lunch--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we got-- oh, we went to her place, to the nana's place. I remember that. But otherwise, I can't, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, describe that. Describe what you'd do at your nana's place.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Well, we went there and-- on-- on Shabbat. And we had a nice Shabbat there. Or on yontif, I used to wait for my mother. I used to go with the youngsters. And on the end of the shul, I bought flowers. And I waited for my mother to-- to give her the flowers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your family go to shul regularly?

speaker: interviewee
text: They went on-- on-- what would you call it? On-- on yontifs-- Sukkot, [INAUDIBLE], Rosh Hashanah, and Yom Kippur. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So how would you describe their religious affiliation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Traditional religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have a lot of friends in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the house? No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Visiting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah. Oh, yeah. Visiting. Friends came for dinner parties. And we went to the other people there. And there was a family called [INAUDIBLE], which was very affiliated with my dad. Knew them from Russia. The only thing that [INAUDIBLE]-- he-- he sort of was trying to hide as a White Russian. He wasn't, you see. But he succeeded, because there was a very-- there was a very affiliated White Russian community in Berlin who sympathized with the Nazis against the communists. But otherwise, we just led a normal, good life. And I was very happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were your best friends before 1937?

speaker: interviewee
text: One fellow called Walter. And I was associated with the Betar. That's a-- that's a youth organization, a Zionist youth organization, which were at that particular time trying to militarize we-- the-- the Jewish youth.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what date are you talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm talking about 1936, '37, '38.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were very much militarized and almost copied the Hitler Youth, only-- only in a-- in a different way, because we were wearing dark brown shirts. So the Nazis thought we were fascist too, so they let us exist. But the Hashomer Hatzair and others, they were forbidden, because they were communists, you see?

speaker: interviewer
text: We were calling himself-- we were calling ourselves the National Zionist Youth-- something National Zionist Youth, you know? And we sent-- a lot of kids went on to-- to Palestine. What about you? How involved were you? And what sort of activities did you get involved in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I went to camps, to youth camps, to Jewish youths camps. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I went to meetings. I went to Jewish meetings, which were there, you know? They were still going on. They were still allowed. And, you know, with the flags and marching, and the blue-white flags, and things like that, and singing songs. And, you know, just that. And that was it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, you know-- but I couldn't get-- I couldn't get a-- I-- I couldn't get a visa to go to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you want to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You tried?

speaker: interviewee
text: We tried. But you see, members of Betar were not given visas to go--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: --because we were a danger to the present government over there, which was under Ben-Gurion, and there was [INAUDIBLE] in that particular time. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's all right. Just keep going. Just keep going.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and-- and we didn't get any visas. So I was hanging around.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family try to leave Germany as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my family did try to leave Germany. As I said, before, they were stateless. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And [SIGHS] my father had made contact with the Costa Rican consul. The Costa Rican consul in Berlin were issuing visas-- not only visas, nationalities and passports as well for money, for big money. But he issued too many, you see? And then all of a sudden, when we were ready to go to Costa Rica with a visa and a passport, he was arrested. So he had the visa and passport?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who arrested him?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Gestapo. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was trying to get the Jews out, you know? But-- for money, naturally. But, you know, the whole thing collapsed, so we couldn't go. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Where German Jews had opportunity to go, because they had German passport with a J on it-- Jude. But still, countries like South America gave those passports visas, where England didn't either, because they were afraid-- they were afraid that the-- there was [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So did your parents keep trying to get passports to go elsewhere after Costa Rica?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you couldn't. There was not possibility.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any-- any memory of Kristallnacht?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, have I got memory of Kristallnacht? Sure I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the 9th of November, 1938, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in the nighttime, we heard terrible noises and screaming of people all over Berlin. They broke all the windows.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, just tell us what you saw and heard.

speaker: interviewee
text: What I saw is a red sky of about 20 synagogues being burned, and screaming people, and-- and then-- and brownshirts running through the streets, arresting Jews and hitting Jews, and dragging them out, and plundering, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: --trucks-- trucks-- on trucks, and away to Sachsenhausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back. Where were you when you heard noises on Kristallnacht?

speaker: interviewee
text: In bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in bed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were woken up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I got a telephone call from Betar to get outside immediately, and I'll be picked up with a truck.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: But the truck was a Betar truck to-- to-- to collect the youth, because we had to run in the shuls [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [SOBBING] to save the torot. And we did run in the burning shuls. I didn't have any fear then. I was young. We ran in and-- and saved whatever we could.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you save? Tell me about where you saved.

speaker: interviewee
text: We ran-- we ran in, and it was already burning. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was burning? Tell me where you went.

speaker: interviewee
text: Inside. Everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which synagogue did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] Strasse.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you ran inside?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was smoking and black. But not only me. There was about four or five of us. And our leaders were standing there. We made a chain to pass on things. The-- the other ones ran right inside, and we were standing halfway.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they saved--

speaker: interviewee
text: And they saved a few torot.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then where did they take the torot?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the truck, hiding them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Betar-- yeah, in the Betar-- in the Betar headquarters, which was like offices and premises and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened that night? Tell us where you were.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, outside, there were people standing with-- with bars and things like that, and started hitting us.

speaker: interviewer
text: They started to hit you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And some of them were bleeding. And some of them were falling off. And we-- we run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you run to?

speaker: interviewee
text: We run to private houses of where-- where our members lived, and headquarters, and so on. And then I came home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened when you got home? What were your parents doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were devastated. And it was also announced on the radio, you know, that the German people were arising after-- after a certain fellow called Von Rath got shot by a guy called Grynszpan. And Grynszpan was a Polish Jew in Paris who show the ambassador.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's what they said on the radio? You heard it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Well, we knew about that. But that was a revenge. They said that was a revenge of the German people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember the broadcast on the radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did your parents do after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had to get out. They're trying to save me. So they made applications, because I had an uncle, which was the-- the brother of my mother lived in Brussels. Now, after the Crystal Night, there was an announcement that Belgium was taking 1,000 Jewish children, Holland was taking 1,000 Jewish children, and Great Britain was-- England was taking 2,000 Jewish children. They had to be under the age of 16. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father was trying to get me in because we had family, we had connections in Belgium. So he succeeded there. And they accepted me on a transport.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the Crystal Night was in the 9th of November. And in December, I remember standing on the Anhalter Bahnhof. This was a huge railway station. There were kids all over the place. And there were nurses from Geneva arriving, from Switzerland. And they put us red crosses around the neck. And we got a number and a name. And I kissed my parents goodbye. They were standing on the-- on the station. And they were hustling us into the train. The train was filled with children. And there was also Gruene Polizei-- that was the green police, border police-- who was on the train, checking papers and things like that. We were issued with papers, all of us. And then the train left the station. [INAUDIBLE] waving, and saw my-- the last time I saw my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take you to get to Belgium from that train?

speaker: interviewee
text: About [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] six hours?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember what you were thinking about on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Blank.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you meet many other children on that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. One girl I could remember. I used to take her out in Belgium all the time. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. I can't remember her name. So you met--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we-- we arrived in Belgium, on the station, in the nighttime-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Gare du Nord, North Station. And there were all stalls set up. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And there were all Jewish women. They were all volunteers. They gave us sandwiches and coffee in the nighttime, and blankets. And they comfort us. There were about 200, 300 [INAUDIBLE]. Families. And then children who had no families, they were adopted to other Jewish families. Took them in the cars and took them home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my uncle was there. He took me home. And that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a bit strange. I hardly know my uncle. Almost like a stranger. He knew me when I was little [INAUDIBLE]. It's my uncle and my auntie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other children in their family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at that stage. No. No. And he had his own house there and his own office. He was well off. He was in-- in electrical and electronic, you know, import. And it was a nice house. And I had a room on the top, on the rooftop. And that was it. And I started to go to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What school did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to a technical school, a purely technical school. But I couldn't speak French. So I had to learn very fast French, and I learned it fast. And I was going to school then. And I-- again, I had a school cap on and things like that. I started all over again. I made a few good friends. One was very, very wealthy. He called himself Bernard. And he was one of the wealthy Jews in-- in Brussels. And I went to jazz bands and things like that. You know, I started to get interested in this, and start to get interested in girls, and dancing, and things like that. And I missed my parents terribly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear from them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they wrote. They wrote.

speaker: interviewer
text: How often were they able to write to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they were writing every week.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you knew they were all right at this stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, yeah. But I was begging my uncle to put up a certain fund, money, to get them out, because you could do it at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was not--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was costing a fortune.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: What month in '39.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. But he didn't-- he was not prepared to put up such a sum. See, you give it to a guide. And that guide guides them to the border and smuggles them over the border. But he didn't want to do it. So that was one resentment. And I said, then I get the money. And I earn-- I pay you off every cent, plus a profit. And he didn't want to do it. He was not very nice to me on the end. Not very nice at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- About your life at school and with friends--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --it was nice?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, it was all right, yes. It was all right. I was very happy in Belgium. Different. It was all different, you know? I had no pressure. And then I-- I continued to school-- schooling, naturally. And then my auntie had a baby. That was my cousin. She comes from Estonia. And her name was Marie Louise. It was a little baby. And that was already going into the 1940s. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And on the 10th of May, 1940, we saw aeroplanes in the sky in the daytime. And people went on the balcony pointing at those aeroplanes. They were German fighters. And they were machine-gunning the streets in the daytime. I saw people pointing at them. And that was the invasion of the Germans in 1940 onto Belgium. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And people prepared to leave. People started packing. They were fighting on the-- on the lines, on the Maginot Line, and they're fighting on the Albert Line.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened? Well, maybe we'll--

speaker: interviewee
text: General mobilization and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe we'll finish this tape now--
